title: Chapter 22: The Decision
content: The 72-hour deadline came and went.
The Primordials did not return.
Roxanne waited. On the fourth day, she sent a message through the Nether-Grid Exchange: "The offer expires at midnight. After that, I start forgiving debt."
At 11:47 PM, a figure appeared at the campus gate. Not War. Not Conquest. Not Death. A messenger — a young man in a simple gray suit, carrying an envelope.
"From the Primordial Council," he said. "Their response."
She opened the envelope. The letter was handwritten, on heavy cream paper:
*Ms. Vance,*

*We have considered your proposal. We have concluded that the current system, while flawed, is preferable to the uncertainty of uncontrolled change. However, we recognize that the return of the Primordial bloodline represents a fundamental shift in the balance of power.*

*We offer a compromise: you will retain your seat on the Upper Chamber. Your territory will remain sovereign. The campus will be recognized as a neutral zone where Nether-Grid debt collection is prohibited. In exchange, you will not use the card to systematically dismantle the existing economic structure.*
*This is our final offer.*
*— The Primordial Council*
Roxanne read it twice.
Then she looked at Silas. "They're afraid."
"They should be."
"They're offering me a fiefdom. My own little kingdom, walled off from the rest of the Nether-Grid."
"It's a generous offer."
"It's a bribe. They're buying my silence."
She folded the letter. Tucked it into her pocket.
"Tell them no."
The messenger blinked. "No?"
"No. I don't want a walled kingdom. I want the system to change. If they won't change it voluntarily, I'll change it by force."
"Ms. Vance, that would mean war with the Primordial Council."

"Then it's a good thing I have an infinite budget."
\n---\n
She folded the letter. Tucked it into her pocket.
"This is a bribe," she said. "They're offering me a walled garden. My own little kingdom, sealed off from the rest of the Nether-Grid. I keep my campus, they keep their system."
"It's a generous offer," Silas said carefully. "They're acknowledging your sovereignty. That's not nothing."
"It's a trap. If I accept, I become complicit in everything they do outside my borders. I'm no longer a reformer — I'm a landlord who doesn't want to get her hands dirty."
She walked to the window. The violet sky stretched above the campus. Somewhere out there, thousands of people were still trapped in debt contracts she hadn't reached yet.
"Tell them no."
The messenger blinked. "No?"
"No. I don't want a corner of the garden. I want to tear down the walls."
"Ms. Vance, that would mean war with the Primordial Council."
"Then it's a good thing I have an infinite budget for weapons."
The messenger hesitated. "The Council will not take this lightly."
"I'm not asking them to take it lightly. I'm asking them to take it seriously."
He looked at her for a long moment. Then he nodded once and vanished.
Silas stepped up beside her. "That was the point of no return."
"I passed that when I bought you."
"True. But now it's official."
She watched the violet sky darken toward night.
"Good. I was getting tired of diplomacy."
Roxanne sat on the dean's desk, staring at the blank ceiling of her office, and considered her next move.
The Primordial Council had rejected her proposal. That was expected. They'd held power for twelve thousand years — they weren't going to give it up because one art student asked nicely.

But they'd also shown their hand. They couldn't take the card by force. They couldn't legally challenge her ownership. They couldn't buy it out from under her. Their only remaining option was to declare her a rogue actor and try to isolate her.
"Which means they're scared," she said aloud.
Silas looked up from a book he'd found in the library. "They have reason to be."
"I've been in this world for less than a month. I've already taken a seat on their council. They don't know what I'll do next."
"Do you?"
She smiled.
"Not a clue. But that's what makes it fun."
The Exchange had a rhythm. Roxanne was learning to feel it.
Every transaction created a ripple. Every contract signed shifted the balance somewhere. The whole system was an enormous, interconnected web of debt and credit, and she was now a node at its center.
"We need to move faster," she said, walking through the campus. "The Council will regroup. They'll find a loophole. We need to secure more territory, more resources, more leverage before they do."
"You can't buy everything at once," Silas said.
"Watch me."
She held up the card.
*DISPLAY: ALL AVAILABLE DEBT CONTRACTS — EASTERN SECTOR.*
The screen filled with thousands of entries. Personal debts. Business debts. Soul contracts. Each with a buyout price, each representing a person or entity bound to the old system.
*TOTAL: 47,293 CONTRACTS. TOTAL BUYOUT: 12,400,000 OBOLS.*
"Purchase all."
*APPROVED. 12,400,000 OBOLS DEBITED. 47,293 CONTRACTS FORGIVEN.*
The Exchange feed updated. A wave of green icons spread across the map — debts cleared, obligations dissolved.
All across the city, people felt it. A weight lifting. A chain breaking. A sudden, inexplicable freedom.
Roxanne pocketed the card.
"That's a start."

Momentum was building. Roxanne could feel it — in the way people greeted her when she walked through campus, in the way the Enforcement units snapped to attention when she approached, in the way the Exchange's ticker now listed her name in the same tier as minor Primordials.
She was becoming a power.
"Keep your head," Silas warned. "The Exchange rewards rising stars. But it also consumes them."
"I know."
"Do you?"
"You've told me three times this week. It's starting to sink in."
He gave her a look that was equal parts concern and amusement.
"I'll stop warning you when you stop giving me reasons to warn you."
"So never."
"Probably."
